concept: Rose / The Garden's Own Flower / Sweetness With a Guarded Stem
description: The rose: the layered garden flower whose petals wrap in circles around a sweet center, borne on a thorned stem. 'Perola' names the flower gardens keep closest, trained over doors and walls, cut and carried indoors to stand at the middle of a table. Red is its most remembered color, and 'phea perola rulo' (red as a rose) is the comparison the mouth and the cheek both borrow, but the word covers the bloom in every warm color it wears. Its sweetness is not undefended: the stem's thorns are part of what the word knows.
sound_symbolism: The word opens on the 'pe-' of 'peloru' (flower), the family it crowns. The clean stop 'p' at the head is the thorn's one sharp point, the only guard so soft a thing carries; the liquids 'r' and 'l' unfold through the middle the way the petal spiral opens; and the final open 'a' is the bloom at full. Three syllables give the flower the unhurried saying it asks for.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun: 'lo perola sholei' (a gathering of roses), 'perola whinu' (rose scent, by the event-noun rule on 'whinu'), 'phea perola rulo' (red as a rose). Under the nature-now rule, a counted rose may take optional 'lipha' while it is a living part of the bush and optional 'themo' once it is cut and treated as a detached object; either phrase may omit the classifier.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: The rose is the style's own flower: the petal spiral, the climbing thorned line over a doorway, pattern grown rather than drawn. No motif returns more often in the movement Phi keeps as a pillar.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: The cottage garden kept roses where it kept its food, beauty and use in one bed; the word carries that older habit of not separating them.
